query: risk factors for coronary artery disease

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Modifiable risk factors have a smaller but still significant role. [11] Yet, only two-thirds of patients receive optimal medication interventions. [18] If this were achieved, there would be a substantial reduction in CAD events. [11] One study observed that those with optimal risk factor profiles had a substantially lower rate of death from cardiovascular events. [19]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD): A 2017 meta-analysis noted that IBD is associated with a higher risk of CAD. However, the results were interpreted with caution due to the heterogeneity of the studies. The risk mechanism was uncertain, but it was thought to be due to a chronic inflammatory state. [63]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Smoking: The Food & Drug Administration (FDA) estimates that cardiovascular disease causes 800,000 deaths and 400,000 premature deaths per year. About one-fifth and one-third of these result from smoking, respectively. A 2015 meta-analysis revealed that smoking resulted in a 51% increased risk (21 studies, RR 1.51, 95% CI 1.41.1-62) of coronary heart disease in patients with diabetes. [36] A separate 2015 meta-analysis revealed that smoking resulted in twice the risk of cardiovascular disease for current smokers and a 37% increase in risk with former smokers among patients > 60 years old. [37] Nonsmokers regularly exposed to second-hand smoke also have a 25% to 30% increased risk of coronary heart disease compared to those not exposed. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the risk factors for coronary artery disease. Assess screening recommendations for coronary artery disease. Identify diet and exercise recommendations for coronary artery disease. Communicate the importance of an interprofessional team-based approach to improve coronary artery disease outcomes. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Given the immense healthcare burden of CAD, modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors were identified to mitigate the resulting economic and disease-related burden. Risk factor identification, combined with advances in medical technology, has significantly decreased CAD mortality rates over the past forty years in Western countries. One public health study that gathered mortality data from 1969 to 2014 concluded that by 2020, the number of heart disease-related deaths would decrease by 21.3 percent for men and 13.4 percent for women. [12] This topic reviews the impact and management of these risk factors. Ever since the Framingham Heart Study, there has been a plethora of data on CAD risk factors. This topic concisely summarizes the most recent systemic reviews and evidence.

retrieved_docs.content: Risk factors for CAD are classified into modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: A 2019 article indicated that age, sex, and race captured 63% to 80% of predictive performance, while modifiable risk factors contributed only modestly. Yet, controlling modifiable risk factors led to substantial reductions in CAD events. [11] Non-modifiable risk factors are discussed first:

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Non-alcoholic fatty liver disease (NAFLD): NAFLD has links to cardiovascular disease. It is also the most common chronic liver disease in developed countries. [53] A 2017 meta-analysis revealed a 77% higher risk (RR 1.77, 95% CI 1.26-2.48) of cardiovascular events and over double the risk (RR 2.26, 95% CI 1.04-4.92) for CAD in patients with NAFLD. [54] A more recent prospective study revealed that patients with NAFLD had greater than double the risk of cardiovascular events. Patients with liver fibrosis had a 4-fold increase. [55]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern -- Socioeconomic Status
retrieved_docs.content: Socioeconomic status is a significant risk factor for cardiovascular disease. Upstream determinants include financial strain, lack of affordable and nutritious food, exposure to domestic violence, and inadequate housing; this is an important consideration to consider, given existing cardiovascular disease risk equations do not capture this. [41]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: In addition to these traditional cardiovascular risk factors, novel risk factors have also been subject to research. These include:

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes mellitus: The Centers for Disease Control (CDC) reports that more than 1 out of every 3 adult patients in the United States have prediabetes, which puts one at risk of developing type 2 diabetes, heart disease, and stroke. The heart disease rate is 2.5 times higher in men and 2.4 times higher in women in adult patients with diabetes compared to those without diabetes. [18] A 2017 meta-analysis indicated that patients with diabetes with an A1C > 7.0 had an 85% higher likelihood (hazard ratio 1.85, 95% CI 1.14-2.55) of cardiovascular mortality compared to those with an A1C < 7.0%. It also revealed that non-diabetic patients with an A1C > 6.0% had a 50% higher likelihood (hazard ratio, 1.50, 95% CI 1.01-2.21) of cardiovascular mortality compared to those with an A1C of < 5.0%. Researchers also reported significant study heterogeneity. [27] Cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of morbidity and mortality in patients with diabetes. [28]

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: Human immunodeficiency virus (HIV): HIV is understood to come with a higher risk of cardiovascular disease and its associated sequelae. [64] A 2018 expert analysis from the American College of Cardiology noted that patients with HIV showed a 1.5 to 2-fold increased risk of CAD. The mechanism, again, was based on a pro-inflammatory state.

retrieved_docs.title: Risk Factors for Coronary Artery Disease -- Clinical Significance -- Smoking
retrieved_docs.content: A 2016 Cochrane review indicated that combined behavioral support and pharmacotherapy had a higher chance of success. [111]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 1931
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is closely linked to several cardiac conditions and risk factors. It is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي), hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), and the presence of a heart murmur (نفخة قلبية). Hypercholesterolemia also has a clinical association with cardiac disorder, underscoring its role as a common metabolic risk factor for both entities. Diagnostic evaluation of coronary artery disease frequently employs coronary computed tomography angiography (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب), which itself is related to the broader concept of coronary disease (مرض التاجي). In the hierarchical structure of cardiovascular terminology, the parent concept “أم دم الشريان التاجي” encompasses cardiac disorder as a narrower element and includes coronary disease as a child, indicating that both the specific cardiac disorder and coronary disease are subcategories within this overarching category. Understanding these interconnections aids clinicians in recognizing the shared pathophysiology, risk profiling, and appropriate imaging strategies for patients with coronary artery disease and related cardiac abnormalities.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 4 -- Community 2832
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating that patients with coronary atherosclerosis frequently exhibit cardiac abnormalities and that the presence of a cardiac disorder may suggest underlying coronary artery disease, a relationship that is important for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 2186
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) and with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني). It also commonly co‑occurs with vascular stiffness (رأب الوعاء). These interconnections indicate that patients with coronary artery disease often have underlying cardiac abnormalities, are at heightened risk for developing congestive heart failure, and frequently exhibit vascular stiffness, all of which are important considerations for diagnosis, risk assessment, and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 1501
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery atherosclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي), and coronary artery atherosclerosis is clinically associated with rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي). This interconnection suggests that individuals with a cardiac disorder may be at increased risk for developing coronary artery disease, and that coronary artery disease may coexist with or predispose to rheumatic heart disease, underscoring the need for comprehensive cardiovascular assessment and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1831
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية). The presence of a heart murmur can serve as an important clinical indicator of underlying coronary artery pathology, reflecting turbulent blood flow that may result from atherosclerotic narrowing or ischemic changes in the coronary vessels. Recognizing this association helps clinicians consider coronary artery disease in the differential diagnosis when a murmur is detected, prompting further cardiovascular evaluation and appropriate management to reduce the risk of adverse cardiac events.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 1495
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with several cardiovascular conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم), which is a major risk factor for the development of atherosclerotic plaque in the coronary arteries. Coronary artery disease also co‑occurs with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), reflecting the progression of ischemic damage to cardiac function. Cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) is frequently employed in the assessment and management of coronary artery disease, as well as in the evaluation of related cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), hypercholesterolemia, chest pain (ألم الصَدْر), and neck pain (ألم في العنق). Cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي) themselves show clinical associations with coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia, and the use of cardiac catheterization, underscoring the interconnected nature of these pathologies. Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is commonly observed in patients with hypercholesterolemia and often prompts cardiac catheterization to rule out or treat underlying coronary artery disease. Neck pain (ألم في العنق) may also be a presenting symptom that leads clinicians to perform cardiac catheterization when cardiac involvement is suspected. Overall, hypercholesterolemia, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, various pain presentations, and cardiac catheterization form a tightly linked clinical network, highlighting the importance of integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 2097
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery atherosclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي), congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), and rheumatic heart disease (مرض قلبي روماتزمي). Conversely, cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery atherosclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي), indicating a bidirectional relationship. These interconnections highlight that patients with coronary artery atherosclerosis frequently present with or are at increased risk for other cardiac conditions—including cardiac disorders, congestive heart failure, and rheumatic heart disease—and that cardiac disorders may also reflect underlying coronary artery disease. Recognizing these linked pathologies is essential for comprehensive cardiovascular evaluation and integrated patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1737
retrieved_docs.content: The cardiac disorder (اضطراب قلبي) is clinically associated with coronary artery atherosclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي). Coronary artery atherosclerosis is evaluated using coronary CT angiography (تصوير الأوعية الدموية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب), and this imaging modality is also linked to coronary disease (مرض التاجي). The coronary arterial blood supply (أم دم الشريان التاجي) has a more specific relationship with the cardiac disorder and serves as the broader concept that includes coronary disease as a subcategory. Together, these relationships illustrate that cardiac disorders are connected to coronary artery atherosclerosis, that coronary CT angiography is a pivotal diagnostic tool for both atherosclerosis and coronary disease, and that the coronary arterial blood supply underlies the pathophysiology of both the cardiac disorder and coronary disease, informing comprehensive patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2830
retrieved_docs.content: Chest pain (ألم الصَدْر) is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia (فرط كوليسترول الدم). Elevated blood cholesterol promotes atherosclerotic plaque development in coronary arteries, which can impair myocardial blood flow and present as chest pain. Recognizing this link enables clinicians to assess patients with chest pain for underlying lipid abnormalities and to consider lipid‑lowering interventions as part of comprehensive cardiovascular risk management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2908
retrieved_docs.content: Inflammatory morphology is recognized as an associated morphological pattern of coronary atherosclerosis and other heart disease, indicating that inflammatory changes in the arterial wall and cardiac tissue are characteristic features of these cardiovascular conditions. This relationship underscores the role of inflammation in the development and structural presentation of coronary atherosclerosis as well as a broader spectrum of heart diseases, highlighting its importance for diagnostic assessment, risk stratification, and therapeutic targeting in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 4 -- Community 2814
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac disorder, coronary artery disease, hypercholesterolemia, and heart murmur are closely interrelated clinical entities. Cardiac disorder is clinically associated with both coronary artery disease and hypercholesterolemia. Coronary artery disease is clinically associated with hypercholesterolemia and also with the presence of a heart murmur. Hypercholesterolemia, in turn, is clinically associated with cardiac disorder. These connections underscore that elevated blood cholesterol contributes to the development of coronary artery disease and other cardiac disorders, and that coronary artery disease may manifest with a heart murmur, highlighting the importance of integrated assessment of lipid levels and cardiac function in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 4 -- Community 2911
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), indicating that patients with atherosclerotic involvement of the coronary arteries frequently develop or coexist with heart failure characterized by fluid overload, a relationship that is important for diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2016
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery disease (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with a range of cardiac conditions and diagnostic procedures. It is linked to cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي), congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني), and heart murmurs (نفخة قلبية), reflecting its broad impact on cardiac function and symptomatology. In addition, coronary artery disease is closely connected to the use of cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية) as a key investigative and therapeutic tool. Cardiac disorders and congestive heart failure are also individually associated with cardiac catheterization, underscoring the procedure’s central role in evaluating and managing these interrelated cardiovascular problems. Together, these associations highlight the intertwined nature of coronary artery disease, various cardiac pathologies, and the pivotal role of cardiac catheterization in patient assessment and care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1491
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery sclerosis (تصلب الشريان التاجي) is clinically associated with cardiac disorders (اضطراب قلبي) and with congestive heart failure (فشل قلبي احتقاني). Both coronary artery sclerosis and cardiac disorders are frequently evaluated using cardiac catheterization (قسطرة قلبية), which is also linked clinically to cellular tissue inflammation (التهاب النسيج الخلوي) and to congestive heart failure. Computed tomography coronary vessel imaging (تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب) is a diagnostic modality related to coronary artery sclerosis and is connected to coronary disease (مرض التاجي). These interrelationships highlight the central role of coronary artery disease in the pathophysiology of cardiac disorders and heart failure, and underscore the importance of imaging and invasive catheter‑based assessment in patient evaluation and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 629
retrieved_docs.content: اضطراب قلبي is clinically associated with تصلب الشريان التاجي, and تصلب الشريان التاجي is also clinically associated with نفخة قلبية. تشخيص تصلب الشريان التاجي frequently relies on تصوير الأوعية التاجية بالفحص المقطعي المحوسب, which in turn is related to مرض التاجي. Additionally, أم دم الشريان التاجي has a narrower relationship with اضطراب قلبي and serves as the parent entity of مرض التاجي, indicating that this specific coronary arterial component links cardiac disorders to coronary disease. Together, these relationships highlight the interconnected nature of cardiac pathology, coronary atherosclerosis, heart murmurs, and the pivotal role of coronary CT angiography in evaluating coronary disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: (ورم مخاطي أذيني) with a risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, and systemic atherosclerotic conditions (peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis) that manifest as aortic calcification and contribute to cerebral hypoperfusion. Collectively, these relationships illustrate an integrated network in which the heart serves as the primary site for electrophysiologic monitoring, invasive diagnostics, mechanical circulatory support, rhythm‑management device therapy, and surgical interventions, while its anatomical constituents and associated diseases are systematically linked to procedural hierarchies and systemic risk factors, underscoring the importance of comprehensive cardiac knowledge for optimal patient care.
